Google just dropped a brand new free update to Google Stitch. This is a massive update that's just dropped and it may be the most important free AI tool for vibe coding in 2026. Here's the problem that it solves. You have an idea for an app or a website or a dashboard or a landing page or a tool, but you can't design, you can't code, you have no team, and hiring someone else gets expensive and takes a long time. That problem is now on. Stitch just turned plain English into real working beautiful software. Not a wireframe, not a rough [music] sketch, an actual functional app with code you can export and deploy in minutes. So they just officially rebranded this whole thing as vibe coding design. You can see an example of how it works right here. And that means instead of learning layers, components, designing systems in Figma, blah blah blah, you just describe what you want like you texted a friend and Stitch builds it. So, in this video, you're going to see exactly how Stitch works. It's free. You're going to build tools that look genuinely insane. Dashboard apps, landing pages, tools. So, if you've never used Stitch before, you can get access right here. It's at available at stitch.withgoogle.com. Now, what you can actually see here is you can switch between web and app. And you can also use the new 3.1 Pro version right here, which is a brand new update and API from Gemini, right? So, you can create apps and you can create websites. This is for the front end, which means the design of it, right? Here's an example of how it works. So for example here I said inside the chat create a beautiful design for an AI jobs board and it designed the whole thing all the pages all the website that we're going to create. We can then export it but I'll come on to that in a second. So what is Google Stitch in plain English? Imagine you could walk up to a really smart designer. You tell them build me a mobile app for my fitness coaching business. Dark colors, green accents, show daily workouts and a progress tracker. And then in under 60 seconds it's done. Beautiful professional. ready to exp that is Stitch, right? It was originally a company called Galileo AI. Google bought them, rebranded it as Stitch, launched it at Google IO last year, and then today they just dropped a major new evolution. So, what does Stitch actually do? Well, you can describe a description of the app or screen that you want. You can see how I've done that over here, right? So, you can just type in your prompt of exactly what you want. Then, from that, Stitch is going to build a highquality professional looking UI. Here's what it looks like, right? You can edit it just by chatting with it. You can export it to Figma, to AI Studio, or to anti-gravity, and you can deploy it live for free. It runs on Gemini's best model, which is Gemini 3.1 Pro Thinking. It's currently free. Before Stitch, you needed a designer, a developer, a project manager, and about 4 weeks. After Stitch, you need 10 minutes and an idea. That is the brand new update. Now, if you're wondering, okay, what's actually changed here? What are the new updates? Let's run through each of them, right? So, number one, voice live mode. You can now speak to your canvas. If you talk to the agent out loud, it listens to responsive audio and updates your design in real time. It can critique your design, build new screens and brainstorm with you like a conversation, right? So, if you want to use the voice mode, you can use that directly inside Stitch just by clicking the start live mode here, right? And you can actually speak to it directly, which is pretty crazy. You've also got 3D workspace. So, Stitch is moving away from a flat screen. The new workspace feels spatial and it appears as a widget, right? So the AI moves around as a widget, which is pretty cool, too. You see how it works right here. Right. Next up, you've got the imagine more screens feature. So once you build one screen, Stitch looks at it and suggests all the screen you're missing, right? On boarding pages, checkout pages, whatever. So for example, here it's broken down all the different parts of the page, right? So for example, the job listings, job details, homepage, application form, and then you can actually change the colors over here. So you've got this synthetic flux where you can actually edit the colors, switch them to your brand, or you can just go back inside the chat and give it another prompt, right? So you can say, okay, use my brand colors. And then if we type in my brand colors right here, it will start editing the design. Use the new methods. And if you want to switch around and move around on the screen, just click the hand gesture here. And then you can move around like that. Right? Pretty simple and easy. And you can see it's editing the brand colors in the bottom left over here whilst it's buffering. You've also got a React app export option. This one of the biggest ones. So Stitch can now generate a fully working React application from your designs. Not a prototype, not a mockup, actual working code. You can take to AI Studio or a developer and deploy it immediately. And then you got MCP export and agent skills for techier people. So it's pretty simple and easy to use. It's free to use. You can use all the latest updates and the designs look pretty awesome. Also, if you add, for example, a new prompt, like if you say, okay, um, change the brand colors, it will create a new version below. So it doesn't delete the old one, which is great. So you can see the progress and what you've done along the way. Now, if you want to export this, you can select which which screens you want to export over here, right? So we can just select multiple ones like this. We can select all of those and then we can say, okay, I want to export this to AI studio or to Google jewels or as a zip file to upload into um anti-gravity. So if we click on build with AI studio over here, what this is actually going to do is now send this over to AI studio, right? So if we enter inside here, we can now attach the files and the images and the export from Google Stitch directly into AI Studio and start building out the app itself. Right? So not only can we design the front end of it for free with Stitch, but then we can deploy and create it using AI Studio and they just work seamlessly together. That was so easy to just in one click create what we want. Now once it's designed the actual application which it's doing over here then we can share or publish it directly from AI studio. So it's super easy and simple just to set up and go from there. So you can see how powerful this is is a huge update. Even just a voice alone would be a huge update in itself. But to do all of these updates in one go it's crazy. It's it's so good and it's so easy to use. So definitely recommend checking this out. I think the cool thing about this is instead of using AI Studio directly to create the apps or instead of using anti-gravity straight off the bat, Stitch is really designed for front end, right? So, it's really good at that. That's what it's programmed to do. It's like one agent dedicated to design. Why is that important? It's important because you're going to get better designs faster and you're going to get things right first time round. Whereas, for example, if you just go straight into AI Studio or into Google Antigravity to create this, the design itself is not going to be as good on the front end. So that's a big difference right here. Now let me talk you through a framework I call the stitch method for using Google stitch right and building things fast with it. And you can use this inside your business or to build anything you want, right? So number one is speak your idea. So you can for example just speak the idea in terms of like what you want. So in this situation I said create a jobs board for an AI automation platform, right? Then you can test multiple variants because Stitch generates multiple variants at once and you pick the best one and then you can iterate with the chat. After that, you can transfer it to code. So you can transfer it to AI studio for deployment like we did over here and it's currently building out the app. So you can see it's pretty much finished actually, which is awesome. Now once you've done that, then you would just connect it to your stack, right? So Stitch doesn't live in isolation. So you could connect it to Superbase, for example, for your database. You could connect it to um Netlefi to deploy the actual application itself. And then the final step is just hand it off or own it, right? You either give the exported code to a developer to finish or you can deploy it yourself with no developer needed.
